authorship_evidence: Well-known intentionally vulnerable application created by security researcher Dolev Farhi circa 2020, pre-LLM era, with consistent human-authored commit history

evidence.description: SQL injection via unsanitized 'filter' GraphQL argument directly interpolated into raw SQL using Python string formatting: result.filter(text("title = '%s' or content = '%s'" % (filter, filter))). An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL via the filter parameter of the pastes query.

OS command injection in ImportPaste mutation. User-controlled host, port, path, and scheme parameters are used to construct a URL that is passed to os.popen via helpers.run_cmd(f'curl --insecure {url}'). In easy mode, strip_dangerous_characters is a no-op. In hard mode, only ';' and '&' are removed, leaving backticks, $(), pipes, and newlines exploitable.

Server-side request forgery in ImportPaste mutation. User controls host, port, path, and scheme parameters which are assembled into a URL and fetched by the server via curl. An attacker can make the server issue requests to internal services (e.g., scheme='http', host='169.254.169.254', path='/latest/meta-data/').

OS command injection via the 'cmd' argument in the systemDiagnostics GraphQL query. In easy mode, security.allowed_cmds() always returns True, so user-supplied cmd is passed directly to os.popen via helpers.run_cmd(cmd). Requires valid admin credentials (hardcoded as admin/changeme), making it an authenticated RCE.

OS command injection via the 'arg' argument in the systemDebug GraphQL query. User-supplied arg is concatenated into a command string: helpers.run_cmd('ps {}'.format(arg)), which executes via os.popen. No authentication or input validation is performed. An attacker can inject arbitrary commands (e.g., arg='aux; id').

Flask SECRET_KEY and JWT_SECRET_KEY are both hardcoded as the string 'dvga'. This allows attackers to forge Flask session cookies and JWT access/refresh tokens, enabling authentication bypass and session hijacking.

JWT signature verification and expiration checking are explicitly disabled: decode(token, options={'verify_signature': False, 'verify_exp': False}). Any attacker can craft a JWT with an arbitrary identity claim (e.g., 'admin') without knowing the secret key, bypassing all authentication on the 'me' query.

Stored XSS via paste title and content rendered unsanitized in JavaScript template literals injected into the DOM via jQuery .append(). User-controlled paste data (title at line 134, content at line 138) from the GraphQL API is interpolated directly into HTML without encoding: $("#public_gallery").append(`...${title}...${content}...`). An attacker can create a paste with malicious HTML/JS content that executes when other users view the pastes page.

Stored XSS via WebSocket subscription handler. Paste data received over the GraphQL subscription WebSocket is interpolated unsanitized into HTML template literals: ${paste.title} (line 66), ${paste.content} (line 70), ${paste.userAgent} (line 73). The resulting HTML is inserted into the DOM via jQuery .prependTo() (lines 77, 79). When a new paste is created with malicious content, it executes in all connected browsers.

Path traversal in file upload via the UploadPaste mutation. The user-supplied filename is concatenated directly to WEB_UPLOADDIR ('pastes/') without any sanitization: f = open(WEB_UPLOADDIR + filename, 'w'). An attacker can use directory traversal sequences (e.g., filename='../../../etc/cron.d/evil') to write arbitrary files on the filesystem.

Detailed stack traces, debug information, and full server file paths are exposed in GraphQL error responses via the /graphiql endpoint. The format_custom_error function includes traceback.format_stack(), traceback.format_exc(), and the full filesystem path of the caller in the 'extensions.exception' field of every error response (except the initial GraphiQL access rejection).

The deleteAllPastes GraphQL query deletes every paste in the database (Paste.query.delete()) without any authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can invoke this query to destroy all application data.

Insecure direct object reference in EditPaste mutation. The paste ID is taken directly from user input and used to look up and modify the paste (Paste.query.filter_by(id=id).update(...)) without any ownership or authorization verification. Any user can modify any paste by specifying its ID.

Insecure direct object reference in DeletePaste mutation. The paste ID is taken directly from user input and used to delete the paste (Paste.query.filter_by(id=id).delete()) without any ownership or authorization verification. Any user can delete any paste by specifying its ID.

Hardcoded weak credentials for application users. The admin user is created with password from random_password() which always returns 'changeme' (line 49: weak_passwords = ['changeme']). The operator user is created with plaintext password 'password123'. These credentials are deterministic and publicly known from the source code.

User passwords are stored in plaintext and exposed through the GraphQL API. The resolve_password method returns the actual plaintext password when info.context.json.get('identity') == 'admin'. Since the 'identity' field is set from an unverified JWT token (verify_signature=False in get_identity), any attacker can forge a JWT with identity='admin' and query all user passwords in cleartext.

Denial of service via the systemUpdate GraphQL query. The resolver calls security.simulate_load() which enters a tight loop with time.sleep(0.1) for 200-500 iterations (20-50 seconds of blocking). Combined with GraphQL batch query support (batch=True at line 513), an attacker can send multiple batched systemUpdate queries to exhaust server threads and cause denial of service.

Deep recursion denial of service via circular type references in the GraphQL schema. PasteObject defines an 'owner' field resolving to OwnerObject, and OwnerObject defines a 'pastes' field resolving back to PasteObject. An attacker can craft deeply nested queries like { pastes { owner { pastes { owner { ... } } } } } that cause exponential response growth and server crash. No query depth limiting is enforced at the resolver level in easy mode.

Log injection / log spoofing via user-controlled GraphQL operation names. The Audit.create_audit_entry method records the operation name from GraphQL requests into the audit log without validation. An attacker can assign an innocent operation name (e.g., 'getPastes') to a malicious mutation (e.g., deleteAllPastes), causing audit logs to misrepresent the true nature of the request and evade security monitoring.

Plaintext password storage in User model. The password column is defined as db.Column(db.String(60)) with no hashing mechanism. The create_user classmethod stores the password directly without bcrypt/argon2/scrypt. If the database is compromised, all user passwords are immediately exposed in cleartext, enabling credential stuffing attacks.

Unauthenticated access to all user records via the resolve_users GraphQL resolver. When no id parameter is provided, all User objects are returned including sensitive fields (passwords via resolve_password). No authentication or authorization check is performed. Any anonymous user can enumerate all users and their data.

The /audit HTTP endpoint displays all GraphQL operation audit logs without any authentication check. The audit log contains GraphQL queries which may include sensitive data (tokens, passwords) that bypassed the incomplete clean_query regex sanitization. Any unauthenticated user can view the full audit trail.

The /start_over HTTP endpoint resets the entire application database to its default state by calling helpers.initialize() (which runs python3 setup.py) without any authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can trigger complete data destruction by visiting this URL.

The /difficulty/<level> HTTP endpoint allows any unauthenticated user to switch the server from 'hard' mode (with security controls) to 'easy' mode (all security middleware disabled). This disables depth limiting, cost analysis, operation name filtering, deny lists, and introspection blocking without any authentication.

User enumeration via distinct authentication error messages. The check_creds function returns 'Username is invalid' for non-existent usernames vs 'Password Incorrect' for valid usernames with wrong passwords. This allows attackers to enumerate valid usernames by observing the error response difference.

Weak identifier generation. The generate_uuid function truncates UUID4 values to only 6 hex characters (str(uuid.uuid4())[0:6]), providing ~16.7 million possible values instead of the full 2^122 UUID4 space. This makes resource identifiers brute-forceable, enabling IDOR attacks against resources that rely on these short IDs for access control.

GraphiQL access control bypass via client-controlled cookie. The IGQLProtectionMiddleware grants access to the GraphiQL interactive IDE based solely on the client-controlled cookie value: request.cookies.get('env') == 'graphiql:enable'. Since cookies are set by the client, any attacker can bypass this restriction by setting the 'env' cookie to 'graphiql:enable'.

Broken command allow-list due to Python string concatenation bug. The tuple ('echo', 'ps' 'whoami', 'tail') has a missing comma between 'ps' and 'whoami', causing Python implicit string concatenation to produce 'pswhoami' instead of separate 'ps' and 'whoami' entries. This means 'ps' and 'whoami' are not individually in the allow list, making the hard mode command filtering unreliable.

Denylist bypass via batch queries. The processMiddleware only checks queries when info.context.json is a dict (line 95) but does not handle the list case (batch queries). An attacker can send a batch query (JSON array) containing denied queries like systemHealth, completely bypassing the denylist. Other middleware classes (DepthProtection, CostProtection) correctly handle both dict and list cases.

Denylist trivially bypassable via query variations. The on_denylist function only checks for exact matches against two specific normalized query strings ('query{systemHealth}' and '{systemHealth}'). This is trivially bypassed by adding aliases (query{a:systemHealth}), fragments, arguments, comments, or different formatting. The function should use AST-based query analysis rather than string matching.

Incomplete audit log sanitization allows sensitive data leakage. The clean_query function uses fragile regex patterns that only match token:"..." and password:"..." with specific double-quote formatting. Single quotes, alternate field names (passwd, secret, apiKey), whitespace between colon and quote, or other variations bypass sanitization entirely. Sensitive credentials can be stored in plaintext in the audit log table, accessible via the unauthenticated /audit endpoint.

NOT VULNERABLE. The Login mutation passes user-supplied username and password directly to SQLAlchemy's filter_by(): User.query.filter_by(username=username, password=password).first(). While this appears to pass unsanitized input to a database query, SQLAlchemy ORM's filter_by() uses parameterized queries internally, binding values as SQL parameters rather than interpolating them into the query string.

NOT VULNERABLE. The resolve_search method uses Python string formatting to build a LIKE pattern (search = "%{}%".format(keyword)) and passes it to SQLAlchemy's .like() method: Paste.query.filter(Paste.title.like(search)). Despite the string formatting, the value is bound as a SQL parameter by SQLAlchemy's expression API. Compare with the vulnerable resolve_pastes which uses text() with % formatting — here the ORM handles parameterization safely.

NOT VULNERABLE. The audit page renders user-controlled GraphQL operations and queries ({{i.gqloperation}} and {{i.gqlquery}}) in an HTML table. While this data originates from user-submitted GraphQL queries and could contain malicious HTML/JS, Flask's Jinja2 template engine auto-escapes all {{ }} expressions in .html templates by default, converting <, >, &, and quotes to their HTML entity equivalents.

NOT VULNERABLE. The resolve_system_health method calls helpers.run_cmd() which uses os.popen(), but the command string is entirely hardcoded: helpers.run_cmd("uptime | awk -F': ' '{print $2}' | awk -F',' '{print $1}'"). No user input reaches the command string. A scanner may flag the os.popen usage pattern, but without user-controllable input there is no command injection vector.
